issues.claim: the customer requirement that drives this codebase did not begin when the team was hired; it predates the founders, predates the first repository
issues.objection: The Sanskrit's 'na tu evāhaṁ jātu nāsaṁ' is a metaphysical claim about eternality of the Self (nityatva). The engineering analog renders 'the customer requirement that predates and outlasts' — this is a register substitution, not a metaphysical claim. The translation explicitly distinguishes itself from metaphysical bravado ('the framing the verse establishes is not metaphysical bravado; it is a discipline of recognition'). Acceptable register-faithful adaptation that preserves the verse's claim of eternality at the level both Advaita and Vishishtadvaita accept (eternality of what is being pointed at).

notes: The engineering layer carefully avoids the verse's central commentarial battleground — whether the plurals (I/you/these kings) commit to genuine ontological multiplicity (Ramanuja) or are concession to body-level plurality with non-dual Self underneath (Shankara). The analog operates at the level both traditions accept: that what is being pointed at (the durable need) is eternal, regardless of how it is counted ontologically. This is exactly the right discipline. The verse's 'sarve vayam ataḥ param' (all of us, hereafter) is preserved as 'today's owners did not summon the underlying need into being, and will not pack it away when they leave' — which preserves both the past-claim and the future-claim of the Sanskrit. The technical claim of nityatva is not flattened into 'embrace change' or 'requirements last forever as buzzword'; the verse's discipline of recognition is preserved. PASS at high confidence.
